title: Episode 4

jokes.text: Fleabag silently admiring the Priest's arms, neck, and general physical appearance while having what should be a serious conversation about death and funerals
jokes.setup: The Priest and Fleabag are engaged in a thoughtful, somewhat philosophical conversation about funerals, death, and the afterlife while apparently fitting or choosing something for the wedding
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's internal monologue keeps interrupting with appreciative assessments of the Priest's body parts — 'His arms,' 'His neck' — entirely at odds with the sacred subject matter
jokes.explanation: This is a masterpiece of structural comedy in miniature. The show has established Fleabag's direct-address confessional mode, so her interjected asides — 'His arms,' 'His neck' — are whispered to us, the audience, while she maintains a composed exterior discussing the afterlife with the Priest. The joke works on multiple levels: the profound gap between the topic (death, the soul, the next life) and her preoccupation (his body); the escalation as 'arms' gives way to 'neck' gives way to 'beautiful neck'; and the fact that it's character-specific — only Fleabag, with her libido-versus-grief-versus-faith arc, would experience this particular collision. The economy is extraordinary: three words at a time do the heavy lifting.

jokes.text: Fleabag accidentally says 'His beautiful neck' out loud instead of keeping it as an internal aside
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been successfully keeping her lustful asides silent — sharing them only with the audience — while discussing funerals with the Priest
jokes.punchline: The internal thought leaks out: she says 'His beautiful neck' aloud, shattering the fiction that she's been holding it together, and then frantically tries to cover it
jokes.explanation: This is the payoff to the running internal-aside gag from joke 1, and it's exceptional. The show's entire grammar — Fleabag speaking to camera while the world remains unaware — is weaponized: the moment her private thought escapes into the diegetic world is both the joke and a genuinely alarming rupture. It confirms the depth of her attraction and her loss of control. 'What?' / 'What?' — her cover-up denial — is perfectly calibrated. The Priest's utterly confused 'OK. Weird.' is devastating in its understatement. This is the kind of structural joke that only works because of how carefully the setup was laid; it earns its 9.

jokes.text: Fleabag, now alone, looks to camera and quietly says 'His beautiful neck' again — a private, helpless repetition
jokes.setup: The 'beautiful neck' moment has just caused an awkward flare-up; Fleabag has fumbled her way out of it
jokes.punchline: Alone, she revisits it — same three words, same dreamy delivery — confirming that she has absolutely no control over this and isn't even trying
jokes.explanation: The rule of three: internal aside (joke 1), accidental externalisation (joke 2), and now the private echo. The laugh comes from the confirmation that she's surrendered entirely. Three words, maximum impact. The economy score near-maxes out because there's nothing to cut. It also works as a moment of character revelation — she's not embarrassed, she's just... smitten, against her will.

jokes.text: Fleabag describes a Quaker meeting to camera: 'It's very intense. It's very quiet. It's very, very... erotic.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is explaining to the audience what a Quaker meeting is — silence, waiting for the Spirit to move you
jokes.punchline: The expected descriptor (solemn, spiritual, boring) is replaced with 'erotic,' delivered with absolute conviction after a pregnant pause
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure ('intense... quiet... erotic') is immaculate. Each word is a step down a ladder toward solemnity, and then 'erotic' kicks the ladder away. What makes it characterologically sharp is that Fleabag means it — the communal silence, the suppressed desire, the enforced stillness surrounded by the Priest: of course it's erotic to her. The joke works as absurdism on first viewing but recontextualizes as psychological truth on rewatch. High quotability: the line stands alone perfectly out of context.

jokes.text: Fleabag in the Quaker meeting, visibly struggling, then suddenly blurting her confession: 'I sometimes worry that I wouldn't be such a feminist if I had bigger tits.'
jokes.setup: The Quaker meeting requires silence unless 'the Spirit moves you to speak.' We watch Fleabag internally wrestling, the silence building unbearably — her face cycling through near-breakdowns — until she can take it no more
jokes.punchline: The Spirit moves her to confess: not a prayer, not a revelation, but a piece of uncomfortably candid self-examination about feminism and breast size
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first great joke. The setup is extended and masterfully paced: nearly a minute of silence and Fleabag's visible internal agony [02:43 to 03:14], building anticipation for whatever she'll say. The punchline subverts on multiple axes simultaneously: the sacred context (Quaker meeting, spirit-led speech) collides with the extremely secular and self-lacerating content; the confession is both genuinely funny and uncomfortably honest about the gap between identity and vanity; and the feminist angle adds a layer of cultural self-awareness. Only Fleabag could say this and have it be revealing rather than just provocative. The word 'bigger' does enormous work — not 'if I were more beautiful' but specifically breasts, specifically size. Borderline 9-territory craft.

jokes.text: The café is revealed to be full of guinea pigs — Fleabag's 'unique selling point'
jokes.setup: Fleabag mentions she should open the café; the Priest asks if he can see it. She hesitates slightly
jokes.punchline: The café is apparently overrun with guinea pigs — multiple characters react with 'The fuck?' / 'Wow' / 'Hello' / 'Oh, you gorgeous little thing'
jokes.explanation: The visual reveal of the guinea pig café is inferred from dialogue reactions — the escalating 'What? / What? / The fuck? / Wow / Hello / Oh, you gorgeous little thing' is clearly structured as a reveal gag. Charming absurdism that grounds the show in its quirky texture. Not a high-craft joke but effective scene-setting comedy.

jokes.text: 'Why so many guinea pigs?' / 'I just thought it'd be a unique selling point.' / 'Which came first, the guinea pig or the guinea pig café?'
jokes.setup: The Priest surveys the improbable number of guinea pigs in the café
jokes.punchline: The Priest frames the business origin story as a philosophical paradox: the guinea pig version of the chicken-or-egg question
jokes.explanation: A neat little joke: the chicken-and-egg paradox applied to guinea pigs is pleasingly absurd, and Fleabag's response ('That is a big old question') treats it with exactly the right mock-gravity. Lightweight but well-structured.

jokes.text: 'What do guinea pigs do?' / 'Um, they are born, they shit themselves with fear, and then they die.'
jokes.setup: The Priest, genuinely curious or making small talk, asks what guinea pigs actually do
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's answer is a brutally compressed life-cycle description that applies equally to humans: born, fear, death — delivered flat, as fact
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent joke. On the surface it's a literal description of guinea pig behaviour, but it's unmistakably a précis of the human condition: birth, anxiety, mortality. The middle term — 'shit themselves with fear' — is what elevates it from bleak to funny; the specificity of the fear, the physicality of 'shit themselves,' and the flat delivery make it land. 'Can I use that at the wedding?' is the Priest's perfect response — immediately clocking its thematic utility. Economy is near-perfect: eight words do existential philosophy. The joke also works as Fleabag characterisation: this is how she sees existence, and it's funnier for being true.

jokes.text: The Priest says 'Can I use that at the wedding?' in response to the guinea pig/life description
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just given a dark existential summary disguised as guinea pig trivia
jokes.punchline: The Priest immediately sees its homiletic value and wants to repurpose it as wedding material — treating a joke as sermon fodder
jokes.explanation: A good reactive beat that extends joke 9 and characterises the Priest simultaneously: he's spiritually attuned enough to hear philosophy in a guinea pig joke, and playful enough to want to steal it. The juxtaposition of 'wedding' and 'shit themselves with fear and die' is the comic engine here.

jokes.text: The Priest notices Fleabag 'disappear' mid-conversation — catches her doing her fourth-wall-breaking dissociation in real time
jokes.setup: Fleabag is about to mention her dead best friend Boo (the 'she what?' moment) and visibly checks out of the conversation — going somewhere else, the way she does to camera
jokes.punchline: The Priest, uniquely, notices: 'It's like you disappear.' / 'What?' He can see what others — and the audience — have been watching all series
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's most sophisticated moments — comedy that functions simultaneously as character revelation and meta-commentary on the show's form. The Priest is the first character to explicitly register Fleabag's dissociative fourth-wall breaks. 'It's like you disappear' is a line that works on the surface (he's observing her emotional evasion) but resonates as a formal joke about the show's grammar. Only this character — intuitive, spiritually attuned, in love with her — would notice. This plants the seed of the Priest's special status. The comedy comes from Fleabag's flustered 'What? Nothing. Nothing!' — she's been caught. High craft, genuine rewatch value, deeply earned.

jokes.text: 'No, stop being so churchy.' / 'I'm not being churchy, I'm just trying to get to know you.' / 'Well, I don't want that.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is trying to probe Fleabag's emotional evasiveness; she's resisting
jokes.punchline: 'Well, I don't want that' — delivered as a flat, sincere refusal of intimacy, which is itself funny because it's so bluntly honest
jokes.explanation: The word 'churchy' is a neat coinage — aggressive but affectionate, and specific to this show's world — and the exchange has a good rhythm. 'I don't want that' lands because it's so nakedly, disarmingly honest as a response to basic human connection. Functional character comedy, well-paced.

jokes.text: 'I think you've played with my guinea pig long enough.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is dismissing the Priest after their emotionally intense and evasive exchange, using the guinea pig as both a literal prop and a displacement activity
jokes.punchline: The line works as a sincere dismissal, a euphemism (arguably), and a comic deflation of the whole charged scene — 'guinea pig' doing enormous tonal work
jokes.explanation: Possibly the best line in the first half of the episode for pure quotability and tonal precision. It's a dismissal, a euphemism, a callback to the guinea pig café, and a very Fleabag-ish way of ending a scene that got too close to real feeling. The understatement ('played with my guinea pig') does double duty as absurdist literalism and as a way of reducing the charged emotional moment to something manageable. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Claire at the funeral — she's accidentally looking spectacularly, impossibly beautiful, and is mortified by it
jokes.setup: Claire is trying to mess up her hair and remove makeup at her stepmother's memorial/funeral gathering
jokes.punchline: 'I just woke up looking amazing — and then everyone's gonna think I got a fucking facial for my mother's funeral.' Her perfect appearance is entirely involuntary and extremely inconvenient
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliant piece of character comedy. Claire — the uptight, controlled, image-conscious sister — is tormented not by looking bad at a funeral but by looking too good, involuntarily. The reversal of the expected vanity joke (she's trying to look WORSE) is perfectly calibrated. 'I'm not wearing any makeup' is the punchline, landing as both absurdist revelation and dark irony. 'Everyone's gonna think I got a fucking facial for my mother's funeral' is a perfectly specific anxiety. Character-specific: only Claire, who cares so much about what people think, would suffer this particular punishment.

jokes.text: 'No matter what I do with my hair, it just keeps falling in this really chic way.'
jokes.setup: Claire is already distressed about looking too beautiful at the funeral; she's trying to fix her hair
jokes.punchline: Even active sabotage fails — her hair is irrepressibly, traitorously chic
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the beauty-at-funeral joke: first it was the face, now even the hair is uncooperative. 'Really chic way' is the right word choice — 'chic' rather than 'nice' or 'pretty' specifies the exact category of attractiveness Claire is accidentally embodying, and it's funnier for the precision.

jokes.text: Multiple mourners arriving to comfort Claire immediately compliment her appearance: 'Gosh, you look gorgeous.' / 'My God, you look well.' / 'Darling, you look wonderful.'
jokes.setup: Claire is desperately trying to downplay her appearance at the funeral; every new arrival adds to her humiliation
jokes.punchline: Every single person who approaches her to offer condolences cannot help but remark on how great she looks — compounding the horror with each iteration
jokes.explanation: The running gag of the compliments escalates the Claire joke effectively: each condolence call becomes another twist of the knife. The structure of 'My deepest condolences — / gosh, you look well' is classically Fleabag in its tonal whiplash. Gets funnier with repetition.

jokes.text: Fleabag spots the Stepmother and accuses her of 'trying to fuck my dad'; Claire snaps 'Can you not think the fucking worst of someone for just a split fucking second? Not everyone is after cock.' — then immediately: 'She's definitely trying to fuck my dad.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag makes a cynical observation about the Stepmother's behaviour; Claire delivers a righteous moral rebuke
jokes.punchline: Claire immediately undermines her own moral position by agreeing with Fleabag: 'She's definitely trying to fuck my dad.' The rebuke was pure performance.
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic structure: the character who delivers the moral high ground immediately abandons it. 'Not everyone is after cock' followed by 'She's definitely trying to fuck my dad' is a perfect self-contradiction, timed with no gap. It characterises Claire as someone whose self-righteous posturing is entirely reflexive and immediately betrayed by her actual instincts. 'Not everyone is after cock' is itself a great line — the absolutism of 'everyone' and the bluntness of 'cock' make it funnier than a euphemism would.

jokes.text: 'Well, she ain't made of wood.' — Fleabag's response to Claire's admission about the Dad
jokes.setup: Claire has just admitted the Stepmother is definitely after their Dad
jokes.punchline: Fleabag defends the Stepmother with cheerful logic: who could blame her? 'She ain't made of wood.'
jokes.explanation: A quick, serviceable joke — the idiom is a well-worn one, but the application (defending a woman for fancying their father at a funeral) gives it a freshness. Efficient beat in a good sequence.

jokes.text: Harry arrives at the funeral: 'Are you OK?' 'Mm-hmm. You? Do you need anything?' 'Uh, no, I'm good, thanks. I've just had a large glass of water.'
jokes.setup: Harry — Fleabag's ex — arrives at the funeral to offer support
jokes.punchline: 'I've just had a large glass of water' — an absurdly specific and entirely irrelevant piece of information offered as an answer to 'Do you need anything?'
jokes.explanation: The awkward specificity of 'I've just had a large glass of water' in a funeral context is the joke — Harry fumbling through social pleasantries and landing on a completely irrelevant hydration update. Good characterisation of Harry as well-meaning but socially graceless.

jokes.text: Harry's trousers are visibly shrunken — left them in the dryer — at the funeral
jokes.setup: Claire notices something wrong with Harry's trousers
jokes.punchline: 'I-I left them in the dryer. / Oh, mate.' Harry is at a funeral in too-small trousers.
jokes.explanation: A solid visual gag inferred from the exchange. 'Oh, mate' is the right response — empathetic, resigned, British. The comedy of small social catastrophes at funerals is well-trodden, but the dryer detail gives it specificity. Competent rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: Mourner approaches to 'comfort' with a lengthy speech about how grief will get worse: 'The hard bit's gonna come in a few weeks... people start to forget... your lovely boyfriends might not be able to cope.'
jokes.setup: A well-meaning mourner approaches Fleabag and Claire to offer support
jokes.punchline: The 'comfort' is actually a systematic preview of coming miseries, delivered with cheerful confidence — ending with the suggestion that their partners will abandon them
jokes.explanation: The joke is the gap between the mourner's intention (comfort) and the effect (cataloguing horrors). The escalation from 'cards stop' to 'people disappear' to 'your partners can't cope' builds nicely. The 'I will always be there for you — always — always' coda, delivered three times, tips from reassurance into something almost threatening. Good cringe comedy.

jokes.text: 'Oh, well, he's my husband, so...' — Claire's correction when the mourner says 'your lovely boyfriends'
jokes.setup: The mourner is delivering the 'your partners might not cope' speech and says 'lovely boyfriends'
jokes.punchline: Claire corrects with 'he's my husband, so...' — the 'so' trailing off with the implication that husbands presumably can't just leave
jokes.explanation: A small, well-placed reaction beat. Claire's correction has the extra layer that we know her marriage is unhappy, making 'he's my husband, so...' subtly poignant as well as funny — the 'so' does ironic work.

jokes.text: 'I'm gonna check on the sausage rolls.' — Fleabag's exit from the mourner's comfort monologue
jokes.setup: The mourner has finished her devastating 'comfort' speech; there is a beat of stunned silence
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's escape: 'I'm gonna check on the sausage rolls.' — the most mundane possible exit from an existential moment
jokes.explanation: Classic Fleabag tonal deflation: from a speech about grief and abandonment to sausage roll logistics. The mundanity of 'sausage rolls' is exactly right. Quick, clean, functional.

jokes.text: The Dad's eulogy about the deceased — he admits 'I found her very difficult' and 'I was jealous of her' as funeral tributes
jokes.setup: Fleabag and her Dad are having a private moment at the funeral; he begins to speak about the deceased
jokes.punchline: His 'tribute' involves admitting he found her difficult, didn't like that she was fun, and was jealous of her — honest but deeply anti-eulogistic
jokes.explanation: The Dad's confession is touching more than funny, but 'That is a lovely thing to say, really' — Fleabag's gentle, slightly startled response to 'I was jealous of her' — is well-placed. The comedy is in the honesty of a funeral where someone admits to ambivalence and envy. Tonal rather than gag-driven.

jokes.text: Godmother arrives and the Dad says 'I'll leave you two' — the girls are visibly reluctant to be left alone with her
jokes.setup: Dad and Fleabag are having a tender moment; the Godmother approaches
jokes.punchline: Dad's exit ('I'll leave you two') is a desertion masquerading as politeness; the sisters' reaction to being abandoned to the Godmother is the joke
jokes.explanation: Competent contextual joke — the audience understands the Dad is abandoning them — but it relies on established character dynamics rather than any formal comedic construction. Functional.

jokes.text: 'She just didn't get the fun gene.' — Dad about Claire
jokes.setup: Dad is trying to get Fleabag to reconnect with Claire, saying 'she loves you'
jokes.punchline: 'She just didn't get the fun gene' — a gentle but devastating characterisation of Claire delivered by her own father
jokes.explanation: The 'fun gene' is a nice coinage — simultaneously scientific-sounding (genetic, inevitable, blameless) and devastating (she simply doesn't have it). Delivered by her own father makes it both funnier and sadder. Efficient.

jokes.text: Dad's pep talk: 'Buck up. Smile. Charm. Off we go.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is crumbling emotionally. Dad is trying to help but is entirely unable to address the emotional reality
jokes.punchline: His entire toolkit for grief is 'Buck up. Smile. Charm. Off we go.' — practical, cheerful, and completely useless for the situation
jokes.explanation: The four-word staccato delivery is the joke — it's a complete emotional response compressed into a military briefing. Very English, very Dad, very funny in its absolute uselessness. High quotability. 'Off we go' as the finalizing command is the best word — it implies motion as solution.

jokes.text: 'I don't know what to do with it. With all the love I have for her. I don't know where to put it now.' / 'I'll take it.' / 'No, I'm serious.' / 'I'll have it. You have to give it to me. OK. It's got to go somewhere.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has a moment of genuine grief — she doesn't know what to do with the love she has for her dead friend now that Boo is gone
jokes.punchline: The Priest immediately, practically, volunteers to receive it: 'I'll take it.' When she protests he's not serious, he insists: 'You have to give it to me. It's got to go somewhere.'
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's most emotionally and comedically complex moments. The joke — 'I'll take it' — works because it treats an abstract psychological problem (displaced grief) as a logistical one with a practical solution. The Priest's insistence ('You have to give it to me. It's got to go somewhere.') is both tender and absurdly literal, as if love were a parcel that can be transferred. The comedy and the emotional truth are entirely fused: he genuinely means it, and meaning it is what makes it both funny and devastating. Only this Priest, in this nascent relationship, says this. High rewatch value because we understand more on second viewing. Justifies its score.

jokes.text: The Priest wakes up at night, startled: 'Oh, fuck. Fuck! Jesus! / Whoa, why are you awake? / It's 9:45. / Oh my God. I thought you were just in my head then. But I mean you were in my head then. But now you're there.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has apparently been awake in the night; he sees Fleabag and is shocked
jokes.punchline: His confession: he had been thinking about her (she was 'in my head') — but now she's physically present, the two realities have collided. His flustered cursing ('Oh, fuck. Fuck! Jesus!') is the joke in itself
jokes.explanation: The joke is the Priest's cascading cursing — 'Oh, fuck. Fuck! Jesus!' — a priest taking the Lord's name in vain while in a state of lust-panic, then immediately catching himself ('Are you OK, Father?'). The admission 'you were in my head' is the key: he's been fantasising about her, and now she's actually there. Character-specific: only a priest trying very hard to resist sin would have this exact reaction.

jokes.text: 'Oh, fuck you calling me Father — like it doesn't turn you on just to say it.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has called him 'Father' in a solicitous tone; he is clearly in a state of flustered desire
jokes.punchline: He calls out that 'Father' turns HER on to say — which is simultaneously an accusation, a deflection, and probably true
jokes.explanation: A bravura line. The Priest, caught in sin-panic, turns the tables: he accuses Fleabag of getting off on calling him 'Father' — which is both projection (he's the one losing control) and plausibly accurate (there is a fetish logic to it). 'Like it doesn't turn you on just to say it' is delivered as a kind of desperate challenge. The sexual frankness from a priest is the shock, and the specificity ('just to say it' — not even doing anything, just the word) is excellent craft. High quotability. Character-integral.

jokes.text: The Priest asks: 'Are you a nostalgic person?' / Fleabag: 'Yeah.' / 'Do you like Winnie the Pooh?' / 'Yeah.' / 'I fucking love Winnie the Pooh. I can't read a Winnie the Pooh quote without crying. Fuck. Piglet.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is in a state of barely-suppressed desire and has asked Fleabag not to move; he tries to redirect the energy
jokes.punchline: His displacement activity is declaring passionate devotion to Winnie the Pooh with unexpected emotional intensity — 'Fuck. Piglet.' — as if Piglet alone encapsulates it
jokes.explanation: Exceptional absurdist comedy. The Priest's strategy to cope with his desire — invoking Winnie the Pooh as an emotional shield — is both funny and strangely touching. 'I can't read a Winnie the Pooh quote without crying' is a perfect detail: sincere, unexpected, and revealing of a man whose emotional life is rich and a little chaotic. 'Fuck. Piglet.' is a masterclass in economy: two words, one a profanity and one a children's character, together an entire emotional collapse. The joke is absurdist but not random — it works because the Priest is genuinely moved, and being moved by Piglet while trying not to be moved by a woman is very funny.

jokes.text: Fleabag begs: 'Please say you were praying for me. I could do with the extra pair of hands. Mine don't seem to have the fucking reach anymore.'
jokes.setup: The Priest admits he was on the verge of a little prayer; Fleabag asks what about
jokes.punchline: She hopes he was praying for her — and the reason she needs prayer is that her own hands 'don't seem to have the fucking reach anymore' — a dark-comic admission of inadequacy/loss of faith in herself
jokes.explanation: A beautifully crafted line: 'Mine don't seem to have the fucking reach anymore' takes a literal image (praying hands, reach toward God) and turns it into a metaphor for Fleabag's spiritual/emotional exhaustion. The profanity ('fucking reach') does tonal work — it's both casual and indicative of real despair. Character-integral: this is exactly the kind of half-joke, half-admission Fleabag makes.

jokes.text: The Priest: 'God help me.' / Fleabag: 'Whoa! / Thank you.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is clearly struggling with desire; he involuntarily says 'God help me'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag interprets this as divine assistance being summoned on her behalf and thanks him/God cheerfully
jokes.explanation: A quick, clean irony: 'God help me' is his SOS about controlling himself around her; she hears it as a prayer dispatched on her behalf and appropriates it. The two-level reading (his desperation vs. her cheerful misinterpretation) is well-structured.

jokes.text: The Priest tells the story of a man who wanted to be a saint so badly he castrated himself — 'just to stop himself... you know... whack!'
jokes.setup: The Priest is apparently trying to redirect his own thoughts — possibly as a warning or distraction technique
jokes.punchline: 'Whack!' — the word and (presumably) accompanying gesture reducing a historical act of extreme religious self-mortification to an extremely casual onomatopoeia
jokes.explanation: The castration story works because of what it reveals: the Priest is in a state of sexual crisis bad enough that he's mentally cataloguing historical deterrents. 'Whack!' is extraordinary economy — it replaces a graphic description with a single sound effect and, we assume, a gesture. The casualness is the joke: he's narrating self-castration as if describing a game of cricket. The subtext (he is trying to talk himself out of desire) makes it funnier and more pointed.

jokes.text: 'Here's to peace.' / 'And those who get in the way of it.'
jokes.setup: They are drinking together; the Priest proposes a toast to peace
jokes.punchline: Fleabag adds '...and those who get in the way of it' — meaning herself, and both of them know it
jokes.explanation: A beautiful little joke that functions as character revelation. Fleabag knows she is destroying his peace — the castration story made the stakes explicit — and rather than be sorry about it, she toasts it. The economy is exemplary: eight words between them, an entire dynamic. The Priest's 'here's to peace' shows his aspiration; her addition shows her awareness of her own disruptive power, and her complicated lack of guilt about it. High quotability.

jokes.text: 'Sometimes I worry I'm only in it for the outfits.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is showing Fleabag his vestments with obvious pride and sensory pleasure — detailing the colour, the quality of 'proper plum' from Italy, the two-year wait before wearing it
jokes.punchline: Self-aware confession: maybe his vocation is partly aesthetic vanity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines for pure quotability and character depth. The Priest has just spent several breathless sentences describing his vestment with the enthusiasm of a fashion devotee — 'proper plum,' 'you can only get proper plum in Italy' — and then this self-deprecating aside arrives perfectly. It's funny because (a) it applies joke 5's feminist/tits logic to a priest ('I worry my identity is partly about aesthetics'), (b) it's probably a bit true, (c) 'only in it for the outfits' as a description of one's priestly vocation is a great absurdist reduction, and (d) the line instantly endears him without diminishing him. High quotability: stands alone perfectly.

jokes.text: 'Sometimes I worry I'm only in it for the outfits.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has been lovingly describing his clerical vestments — the trip to Rome, the two-year wait, the perfect shade of plum — with genuine aesthetic passion
jokes.punchline: He undercuts it with the admission that his priestly vocation might just be a fashion motivation
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect line. The buildup — his entirely genuine, detailed, delighted enthusiasm for the vestments (going to Rome, the plum that can only be found in Italy) — earns the punchline completely. The self-deprecating doubt 'I'm only in it for the outfits' is funnier for being offered as a real concern rather than a joke. Works as character revelation (he IS that passionate about the clothing), as comedy, and as a moment of vulnerability. Extremely quotable. The craft is in the setup — the longer he talks about the garments, the better the punchline.

jokes.text: 'I mean, your stuff is lovely, too.' — Priest to Fleabag, after admiring his vestments
jokes.setup: The Priest has been waxing lyrical about his sacred vestments
jokes.punchline: He awkwardly pivots to complimenting Fleabag's presumably ordinary clothes — 'your stuff is lovely, too' — an endearingly clumsy social grace
jokes.explanation: A small character beat: after the grandeur of Italian plum vestments, he gallantly extends the compliment to her ordinary clothes. The absurd scale difference (Italian liturgical garments vs. whatever Fleabag is wearing) makes 'your stuff is lovely, too' endearing and mildly funny. Good texture.

jokes.text: 'And you make me tell you all my secrets so you can ultimately trap and control me?' / 'Yeah.' — Fleabag's characterisation of confession
jokes.setup: The Priest has just described confession as: 'You tell me what's weighing on your heart and I listen without judgment and in complete confidence'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag summarises it as: make her tell her secrets so he can trap and control her — and the Priest cheerfully agrees: 'Yeah.'
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the Priest's 'Yeah' — his willingness to accept her paranoid framing rather than correct it. Fleabag's description of confession as a control mechanism is funny as cynicism; the Priest agreeing rather than defending the sacrament is the actual punchline. Good economy and character integration: his 'Yeah' says he understands her better than to bother arguing.

jokes.text: Fleabag refuses to say 'Bless me Father for I have sinned': 'I'm not gonna say that.' / 'What? Very good.' / Then: 'I'm not Catholic.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is walking Fleabag through the ritual opening of confession
jokes.punchline: 'I'm not gonna say that' / later 'I'm not Catholic' — Fleabag's resistance to every procedural element of the ritual she's voluntarily attending
jokes.explanation: Consistent characterisation: Fleabag participates in rituals on her own terms. 'I'm not gonna say that' about the prescribed formula is funny for its directness; 'I'm not Catholic' as a later protest, when she's already in the confessional, lands as a classic non-excuse. Good rhythm in the exchange.

jokes.text: 'Won't I catch fire or something?' / 'If you did, it would confirm my faith, so let's try it.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag hesitates before entering the confessional
jokes.punchline: The Priest's response to her fear of spontaneous combustion: he'd actually welcome it as proof of God's existence — so it's a win either way
jokes.explanation: The Priest's theological pragmatism is the joke: her burning would be evidence of divine judgement, which would be great for his faith project. He's not discouraging her, he's genuinely interested in the experimental outcome. The structure is clean: her fear, his counter-argument, the implication that her faith concerns are actually useful to him. Highly quotable, character-specific. Earns its 8.

jokes.text: Fleabag's confession list: 'I've stolen things. I've had a lot of sex outside of marriage. And once or twice inside someone else's. Uh, there's been a spot of sodomy. Then, much masturbation — a bit of violence, and of course the endless fucking blasphemy.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has asked for Fleabag's sins; she begins listing them
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from standard (theft, premarital sex) through increasingly specific sins ('inside someone else's marriage,' 'a spot of sodomy,' 'much masturbation') to 'the endless fucking blasphemy' — which is itself an act of blasphemy
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The list is expertly structured: starts with ordinary sins (theft, sex), escalates with specificity ('inside someone else's'), takes a sharp turn into the archaic register ('a spot of sodomy' — 'spot' doing extraordinary work), peaks with 'much masturbation' (the quantifier 'much' is perfect), adds 'a bit of violence' almost as an afterthought, and then the capstone: 'the endless fucking blasphemy' — which commits the very sin it names, in real time, in a confessional. The self-referential irony of the closing blasphemy is exceptional. Economy is superb: each sin is compressed to minimum words. Character-integral: this is exactly the register and candour only Fleabag has.

jokes.text: 'Uh, there's been a spot of sodomy. Then, much masturbation, a bit of violence, and of course the endless fucking blasphemy.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is continuing her confession list
jokes.punchline: The catalogue escalates through sodomy, masturbation, violence and lands on 'the endless fucking blasphemy' — where the word 'fucking' is itself blasphemy, making the sentence self-illustrating
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of self-reference. 'The endless fucking blasphemy' is itself an act of blasphemy — the profanity performing the sin it's confessing. The qualifiers — 'a spot of,' 'much,' 'a bit of,' 'endless' — are calibrated with precision, escalating from polite understatement to resigned exhaustion. 'Of course' before 'the endless fucking blasphemy' implies it goes without saying, which is the perfect note of self-awareness. Earns 8+ because the self-illustrating nature of the sentence is a formal achievement, not just a good line.

jokes.text: 'I want someone to tell me what to wear in the morning.' / '...I want someone to tell me what to wear every morning.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has asked what she wants; Fleabag begins a confession of existential neediness
jokes.punchline: The correction from 'in the morning' to 'every morning' — the escalation from a single instance to a permanent condition — lands the joke while grounding the speech in genuine character truth
jokes.explanation: The correction ('every morning') is a perfect escalation beat. The first version sounds like a mild wish for simplicity; the corrected version reveals the depth of her desire for surrender. This is the hinge of the monologue — the moment where the comedy tips into something more genuinely exposing. The specificity of 'what to wear' as the first and most mundane of life's choices makes the subsequent list ('what to eat, what to like, what to hate') feel both comic and truthful.

jokes.text: 'I want someone to tell me what to wear in the morning.' / '...I want someone to tell me what to wear every morning.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has asked what she wants; she begins with something seemingly modest
jokes.punchline: The correction from 'in the morning' to 'every morning' is the joke — the modest request becomes an endless total dependency in a single word change
jokes.explanation: A quietly devastating escalation joke. 'I want someone to tell me what to wear in the morning' — the Priest's face presumably starts to say 'OK, that seems manageable...' — then: 'every morning.' The correction expands the scope infinitely. This is the opening line of Fleabag's great confession speech, and it works both as comedy (the absurdity of wanting total life-management) and as the most honest thing she has ever said. The structure of setup/correction is nearly perfect. Approaches the 8.0 anchor in precision and economy.

jokes.text: 'Kneel.' / 'What?' / 'Kneel.' / (long silence — Fleabag kneels)
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just delivered her entire 'tell me how to live' monologue and asked him — desperately, sincerely — to 'just fucking tell me what to do, Father'
jokes.punchline: He obliges, minimally: 'Kneel.' — one word, repeated. The comedy is in the anti-climax: she asked for total existential guidance; he gives her one instruction.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke, and one of the finest in the series. After a minute-long monologue in which Fleabag has stripped herself bare and begged for total life guidance, the answer comes in one word. 'Kneel' is the payoff to everything: it IS an instruction (she asked for one); it is an act of religious submission (he gives her what his world offers); it is also an act of genuine care (he does what he can within the limits of what he is); and it is funny because of its radical understatement — this is all he has, and it's actually something. The economy score maxes out: one word does more work than most speeches. The pause before and the long silence after the second 'Kneel' are as important as the word itself. This moment is both funny and profoundly moving — and the coexistence of both is the highest achievement of this kind of comedy.

jokes.text: 'This is a skirt and trousers?' — Fleabag's reaction to her outfit during/after the church scene
jokes.setup: The episode has just ended with the extraordinary kneeling/Kyrie sequence — transcendent, quiet, sacred
jokes.punchline: The final line shatters the mood with a completely mundane clothing concern: Fleabag breaks the spell by noticing (or someone noticing) her outfit
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback and tonal deflation. Fleabag asked to be told what to wear — and here, post-transcendence, the first thing she notices is a wardrobe query. The callback to 'what to wear every morning' gives this throwaway line a structural weight it wouldn't otherwise have. It also restores the show's tonal register after the Kyrie sequence, which risked becoming purely dramatic. The economy is exquisite: five words undoes three minutes of sacred music and religious feeling, in the most Fleabag way possible. The 'Sorry, sorry' following suggests the question may have been audible — a tiny cringe beat to end on.

tmdb_overview: The priest takes Fleabag for some quiet reflection leading to an unexpected revelation. After a day spent looking back on painful memories, Fleabag searches for solace, only to find more trouble.
